questions.questionText: In context to the Indus Valley Civilization, consider the following information: Archaeological Site Located on the Banks of River Well Known for I. Kalibangan Ghaggar river Furrows of ploughed fields II. Dholavira Bhogava river Advanced water management system III. Mohenjodaro Indus river Dancing girl sculpture made of bronze IV. Rakhigarhi Ravi river Bead-making workshops In how many of the above rows is the given information correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. **Some of the important cities of Indus Valley Civilization are Mohenjodaro, Harappa, Allahadino, Chanhudaro, Lothal, Surkotada, Dholavira, Kalibangan, Banawali and Rakhigarhi.**Information in Row I is correct. Kalibangan is located on the bank of Ghaggar river**. The settlement of Kalibangan is located in Rajasthan along the dried up bed of the river Ghaggar. The most significant discoveries here have been those of fire altars.** Furrows of ploughed fields at Kalibangan**have been discovered. The evidence of a furrowed field in Kalibangan indicates that the Harappans were using some sort of wooden plough.**Information in Row II is incorrect. Dholavira is situated on the bank of Luni river.**It is located in the Kutch region. The people at Dholavira had developed a complex water management system on a large scale in order to make it sustainable. At Dholavira, at least six large reservoirs were built with stones or even cut into the rock. Most of them were connected through underground drains for efficient water harvesting and distribution. The town plan is unique. Instead of two, it has three areas: citadel-bailey complex, middle town and a lower town located within the same fortified complex. Lothal is situated along Bhogava river (a tributary of Sabarmati).**Information in Row III is correct.**Mohenjodaro is located in the Larkana district of Sind on the bank of** the river **Indus. **One of the best known artefacts from the Indus Valley is this approximately four-inch-high Bronze/copper figure **of a dancing girl. Found in Mohenjodaro,**this exquisite casting depicts a girl whose long hair is tied in a bun. Bangles cover her left arm, a bracelet and an amulet or bangle adorn her right arm, and a cowry shell necklace is seen around her neck. Her right hand is on her hip and her left hand is clasped in a traditional Indian dance gesture. She has large eyes and a flat nose. This figure is full of expression and bodily vigour and conveys a lot of information. Important findings from Mohenjodaro include:figure of the bearded man, Dancing Girl of bronze, Great Bath, Assembly Hall,**Information in Row IV is incorrect. **Rakhigarhi is situated on the bank of the** now dry Saraswati river in Haryana. ** A unique feature at Rakhigarhi was that in the cemetery the female burials often had more burial goods than the male burials.
Bead-making workshops have been recovered from Mohenjodaro, Chanhudaro, and Lothal.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: Which of the following statements characterize the economic life of the people of the Later Vedic Period?
I. Mixed farming was widely practised by the people.
II. The Northern Black Polished Ware (NBPW) was the characteristic pottery of this period.
III. People widely used punch marked coins as a medium of exchange.
IV. Iron is believed to have been first used during this period.
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.questionText: Which of the following statements characterize the economic life of the people of the Later Vedic Period? I. Mixed farming was widely practised by the people. II. The Northern Black Polished Ware (NBPW) was the characteristic pottery of this period. III. People widely used punch marked coins as a medium of exchange. IV. Iron is believed to have been first used during this period. Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. **The period from 1000 BCE to 600 BCE is referred to as the Later Vedic Period.**

Statement I is correct.**During the later Vedic phase, agriculture became the mainstay of the Vedic people. Pastoralism was no longer the main subsistence activity of the people as it was in the Early Vedic period.**Mixed **farming which included cultivation and herding was the occupational norm of this period. **Many rituals were introduced to initiate the process of agriculture. It also speaks of ploughing with yokes of six and eight oxen. The buffalo had been domesticated for agricultural purposes. This animal was extremely useful in ploughing the swampy land.**

Statement II is incorrect. Northern Black Polished Ware (NBPW)**is associated with the ‘Second Urbanization’ in India. It is an important pottery tradition of the Indian subcontinent. The time range is 600 BCE-100 BCE. It was during this period that processes related to the formation of states and emergence of urbanism in the Ganga valley crystallized. The Iron Age of north India is broadly associated with Painted Grey Ware (PGW) culture. Broadly the time period of PGW culture is between**1000-60**0 BCE.**

Statement III is incorrect. **The first documented coinage is deemed to start with 'Punch Marked' coins issued** after **the later vedic period **between the 6th century BC and 3rd century BC. These coins are called 'punch-marked' coins because of their manufacturing technique. Mostly made of silver, these bear symbols, each of which was punched on the coin with a separate punch.**

Statement IV is correct. **During the later Vedic phase, the beginning of the use of iron occurred around 1000 BC**. **Archaeology has shown that iron began to be used around 1000 BC which is also the period of later Vedic. **

questions.subject: Science & Technology
statementLines: Consider the following statements regarding the Biological Weapons Convention (BWC):
I. It is the first multilateral disarmament treaty banning an entire category of weapons of mass destruction (WMD).
II. The Organisation for the Prohibition of Chemical Weapons (OPCW) is responsible for the implementation of the
Convention.
III. India has neither signed nor ratified the Convention.
Which of the statements given-above is/are correct?
questions.questionText: Consider the following statements regarding the Biological Weapons Convention (BWC): I. It is the first multilateral disarmament treaty banning an entire category of weapons of mass destruction (WMD). II. The Organisation for the Prohibition of Chemical Weapons (OPCW) is responsible for the implementation of the Convention. III. India has neither signed nor ratified the Convention. Which of the statements given-above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. **

Statement I is correct. T**he Biological Weapons Convention** is the first multilateral disarmament treaty that banned **an entire class of weapons of mass destruction (WMDs).**The treaty prohibits the development, production, acquisition, stockpiling, transfer, and use of biological weapons.**The Biological Weapons Convention (BWC) entered into force on 26 March 1975. **

Statement II is incorrect. **The Organisation for the Prohibition of Chemical Weapons is the** implementing body for **the Chemical Weapons Convention. **Organisation for the Prohibition of Chemical Weapons is an international organization established by the CWC in 1997 to implement and enforce the terms of the CWC.**The State parties to the Biological Weapons Convention (BWC) are primarily responsible for the implementation of **the Convention.**BWC States Parties have strived to ensure that the Convention remains relevant and effective, despite the changes in science and technology, politics and security since it entered into force. Throughout the intervening years, States Parties have met approximately every five years to review the operation of the BWC. Between these Review Conferences, States Parties have pursued various activities and initiatives to strengthen the **effectiveness and improve the implementation of the Convention.**A total of eight Review Conferences have taken place since the first one in 1980.**Activities of the BWC Implementation Support Unit support efforts to strengthen the implementation of the **Convention. **The BWC Implementation Support Unit regularly updates a document that provides information on additional agreements which (a) interpret, define or elaborate the meaning or scope of a provision of the Convention; or (b) provide instructions, guidelines, or recommendations on how a provision should be implemented.**

Statement III is incorrect.**According to the United Nations Office for Disarmament Affairs (UNODA), the BWC presently has 189 states parties. India is one of the 189 States Parties to the Biological Weapons Convention (BWC).**India signed and ratified the BWC in 1974. **

Knowledge Base: Biological weapons consist of a weaponised agent and a delivery mechanism. A weaponised agent can be any disease-causing organism or toxin that can be modified to make it more harmful, lethal or suitable for mass dissemination.
Delivery systems can be in the form of constructed missiles, hand grenades, rockets, spray-tanks, sprays, brushes, or injection systems. BWC bans the development of both weaponised agents and delivery systems.

statementLines: "This School of thought belongs to Mahayana Buddhism. It propounds that the consciousness (mind) alone is ultimate reality, and denies the independent existence of external objects.”
The above description reflects the characteristics of which of the following schools of Buddhism?
questions.questionText: "This School of thought belongs to Mahayana Buddhism. It propounds that the consciousness (mind) alone is ultimate reality, and denies the independent existence of external objects.” The above description reflects the characteristics of which of the following schools of Buddhism?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. **Since Buddha did not give answers to many of the vital philosophical issues, his followers tried to find answers to such questions. These attempts, in turn, gave rise to different philosophical directions and schools.**

Option a is incorrect. **The Vaibhasika School**belongs to Hinayana Buddhism.**The Vaibhasika derives its name from its exclusive emphasis on a particular commentary, the abhidhammamahavaibhasa of Abhidhamma. In the true spirit of the doctrine of dependent origination, the Vaibhasika holds that reality is pure flux and change. The**Vaibhasika **speaks about the existence of the mental and the non-mental realities. **It teaches that we can really know the external entities and the world outside.**

Option b is correct. **The**Yogacara School belongs to Mahayana Buddhism**The core of the doctrine of the Yogacara is that** consciousness (mind) alone is ultimately real**. Thus**, external objects are regarded as unreal. **For Yogacara, all internal and external objects are ideas of the mind. Thus, it is impossible to demonstrate the independent existence of external objects. The philosophers of this school are known as the advocates of consciousness (vijnanavada). Yogacara offers another argument to deny the**independent existence of the external object, **which seems to be a criticism of the Sautrantika and the Vaibhasika schools. It argues that if there is an object outside, it must be indivisible, partless, and atomic, or divisible and composite. If it is the former, it cannot be perceived since atoms are too minute. If it is composite, we cannot perceive all the parts and the sides of the object simultaneously. Thus, in either case the existence of the external objects is denied.**

Option c is incorrect. **The Madhaymika School also belongs to Mahayana Buddhism**. The Madhaymika avoids all the **extremes, **such as, eternalism and annihilationism, self and non-self, matter and spirit, unity and plurality, and identity and difference. The founder of this school is supposed to be Nagarjuna of the second century CE. One of the most important insights of Nagarjuna is the origin and nature of philosophy and philosophical conflicts. For him, knowledge is the means by which man seeks to unite the self and the other. The Madhaymika claims that concepts and conceptual systems are relative to each other. They cannot stand by themselves and generate truths. Consequently, no system can claim absolute truth and validity.**

Option d is incorrect.**The Sautrantika School gives greater importance to the authority of the sutra-pitaka of the Pali Canon. This school also belongs to Hinayana Buddhism.**The Sautrantika subscribes to both the mental and the non
- **mental reality. **

questions.subject: History
statementLines: Consider the following rulers of Magadha kingdom in ancient India:
I. Udayin
II. Shishunaga
III. Mahapadma Nanda.
Which of the following is the correct chronological order of the reign of the above rulers, starting from the earliest?
questions.questionText: Consider the following rulers of Magadha kingdom in ancient India: I. Udayin II. Shishunaga III. Mahapadma Nanda. Which of the following is the correct chronological order of the reign of the above rulers, starting from the earliest?
questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. **The major dynasties that played key roles in the rise and expansion of Magadha were the Haryanka, Shishunaga, Nanda, and Maurya dynasties.**Option I:**Ajatashatru of Haryanka Dynasty is said to have ruled over Magadha from 492 BCE to 460 BCE. He was succeeded by** Udayin (460-444 BCE). **During Udayin’s reign the Magadhan kingdom extended in the north to the Himalayan ranges and in the south to the Chhota Nagpur hills. He is said to have built a fort on the confluence of the Ganga and the Son.**Option II: Shishunaga,**a viceroy at Benaras, was acquired the throne of Magadha i**n 413 BCE**. The rule of the Shishunaga dynasty was of short duration. The most important achievement of Shishunaga was to defeat Avanti (Malwa) and make it a part of Magadha.**Option III: **Shishunaga dynasty was of short duration and gave way to the rule of the Nanda dynasty headed by the usurper Mahapadma Nanda. After deposing the last ruler of the Shishunaga dynasty,**Mahapadma Nanda assumed **control of Pataliputra around 372 BCE**. It was during the rule of the Nandas in Magadha and the Ganga plains as a whole that the invasion of Alexander took place in north-west India.
